title: IRNA and Foreign Affairs Agree: The US Is Losing the Iran War
subtitle: A cross-ideological convergence, a Saudi secret buried in Brazilian Portuguese, and the Beijing summit that changes everything
body_md:
[IRNA English](https://en.irna.ir/photo/86151400/US-cannot-win-war-with-Iran-says-Al-Jazeera), citing Al Jazeera, published this on May 11: "there is absolutely no military path to victory. The only option, and the one the US appears to be taking, is retreat, while Iran maintains control over the Strait of Hormuz." That sentence comes from Iranian state propaganda, which has every incentive to amplify it. What makes it arresting is the independent corroboration. [Foreign Affairs](https://www.foreignaffairs.com/iran/winners-and-losers-iran-energy-shock) — the flagship journal of the US foreign policy establishment — concluded in the same cycle: "Beijing, then, may emerge from the war in Iran as its winner — and Washington as its ultimate loser." Two months into an American military campaign against Iran, the most credible adversary voice and the most credible establishment journal have reached the same verdict.

The Anglophone wire consensus centered on impasse. [CBC World](https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/iran-us-ceasefire-drone-attacks-9.7194290?cmp=rss) led with Trump calling Iran's latest ceasefire response "totally unacceptable"; [Iran International](https://www.iranintl.com/en/202605112092) reported Tehran's foreign ministry insisting "We are not bullies; we stand against bullies." Across Canada, Kenya, UK, and Pakistan — among the most lexically convergent Anglophone buckets in this corpus — both sides claim reasonableness, both sides claim the other is the obstacle, and the negotiations via Pakistani mediators remain in limbo.

The Kissinger frame arrived from Asia, and only from Asia. [Asia Times](https://asiatimes.com/2026/05/the-false-promise-of-a-us-iran-quick-fix/) alone invoked the 1973 Paris Peace Accords as a cautionary template: "Kissinger eventually conceded that deal-making was usually temporary, unless military and political realities changed." The Trump-Xi summit in Beijing (May 13–15) runs through multiple buckets — [Haaretz](https://www.haaretz.com/us-news/2026-05-11/ty-article/.premium/trump-reportedly-heads-to-beijing-seeking-xi-jinpings-help-to-end-iran-war/0000019e-15e0-d969-adff-15ed10e40000) reports Trump is expected to press Xi for help ending the war and reopening Hormuz — but Asia Times alone treats the summit as a Kissingerian linkage opportunity requiring structural ambition rather than a transactional ask.

The energy cascade has already reached Scandinavia. [The Local Sweden](https://www.thelocal.se/20260505/sas-cancels-1200-flights-in-may-due-to-fuel-shortages) reported that SAS was cancelling nearly 1,200 flights in May due to soaring jet fuel prices from the Hormuz closure — the only corpus entry tracing the crisis through consumer aviation. [El País](https://english.elpais.com/economy-and-business/2026-05-05/strait-of-hormuz-fills-up-with-loaded-tankers-without-a-clear-destination.html) documented Gulf countries loading crude onto tankers anchored at sea as onshore storage reaches capacity. [Foreign Affairs](https://www.foreignaffairs.com/iran/winners-and-losers-iran-energy-shock) catalogued the downstream: the Philippines declared a national energy emergency on March 24, Zambia suspended fuel levies at a cost of $100 million, Slovenia is rationing fuel. [Deutsche Welle](https://www.dw.com/en/the-weaponization-of-shipping-channels/a-77108496?maca=en-rss-en-all-1573-rdf) generalized upward — the crisis has focused attention on the Taiwan Strait and Strait of Malacca as additional chokepoints subject to the same geopolitical leverage. [RT Africa](https://www.rt.com/africa/639644-somali-pirates-houthis-ties-red-sea/?utm_source=rss&utm_medium=rss&utm_campaign=RSS) tracked an African vector invisible to most of the corpus: Houthi transfer of GPS targeting technology to Somali pirate networks as a documented side effect of the war.

The cross-ideological convergence between IRNA and Foreign Affairs rests on the same observation from opposite directions. IRNA reads American restraint as proof of Iranian strategic strength. Foreign Affairs reads it as Chinese opportunity and American strategic failure. The joint conclusion — two months of military pressure have not restored Hormuz transit or compelled Iranian capitulation, and the costs are accumulating faster than the leverage — is the same regardless of which premise you start from.

Buried in Brazilian Portuguese, and largely absent from Anglophone coverage, is one consequential diplomatic fact. [Folha de São Paulo](https://redir.folha.com.br/redir/online/mundo/rss091/*https://www1.folha.uol.com.br/mundo/2026/05/arabia-saudita-negou-espaco-aereo-aos-eua-e-frustrou-plano-de-trump-para-navios-no-estreito-de-hormuz.shtml) reported that Saudi Arabia denied the United States airspace rights after Trump announced naval convoy escorts through Hormuz — frustrating the plan before it launched. South Korea, despite heavy LNG dependence on Gulf shipping, ran domestic fuel price caps in Yonhap rather than diplomatic commentary. The Philippines, the world's first declared energy-emergency state, published diesel price rollback tables in place of geopolitical analysis.

The Beijing summit opens May 13. China is Iran's largest oil customer and, as Asia Times notes, "holds real cards in Tehran, but it has so far avoided heavy pressure." Whether Xi applies leverage — and what Washington offers in return — is the singular variable on which the structural impasse either resolves or calcifies.
